name: Fergus mac Róich
description: Fergus mac Róich is a prominent hero in the Ulster Cycle of Irish mythology, renowned for his immense strength, wisdom, and generosity. He was once the king of Ulster but relinquished his throne to Conchobar mac Nessa after being tricked, a decision that would shape much of his life and the ensuing conflicts within the tales. Fergus is often depicted as a larger-than-life figure, both physically imposing and possessing a great appetite for food and drink. Despite his noble qualities, he is also portrayed as being easily manipulated, particularly by women. His loyalty to Ulster is constantly tested, leading him to eventually side with Queen Medb of Connacht in the Táin Bó Cúailnge (Cattle Raid of Cooley), where he acts as a foster-father and protector to the young hero Cú Chulainn, though he is ultimately unable to prevent the devastation wrought upon Ulster. Fergus is a complex character, embodying both heroic virtues and human flaws, making him a compelling and enduring figure in Irish legend. His role in the Táin Bó Cúailnge is particularly significant, showcasing his internal conflict and the tragic consequences of his choices. He is remembered as a powerful warrior, a wise counselor, and a tragic figure whose fate is intertwined with the destiny of Ulster. His legacy continues to resonate in Irish literature and cultural memory.
subtitle: Wise, strong, betrayed, and conflicted hero.
achievements: Warrior in the Táin Bó Cúailnge

keyMyths.title: Loss of the Kingship
keyMyths.description: Fergus was tricked into relinquishing the kingship of Ulster to Conchobar mac Nessa through deceit and manipulation. This event marks a turning point in his life and sets the stage for future conflicts.
keyMyths.source: Various versions of Ulster Cycle tales

keyMyths.title: The Táin Bó Cúailnge
keyMyths.description: Fergus joins Queen Medb's forces against Ulster in the Táin Bó Cúailnge, driven by a sense of injustice and loyalty to Medb. He is a key figure in the conflict, battling against his former countrymen.
keyMyths.source: Táin Bó Cúailnge (The Cattle Raid of Cooley)

keyMyths.title: Fosterage of Cú Chulainn
keyMyths.description: During the Táin, Fergus acts as a foster-father and protector to the young hero Cú Chulainn, demonstrating a complex relationship of respect and affection amidst the conflict.
keyMyths.source: Táin Bó Cúailnge (The Cattle Raid of Cooley)

sources.author: Thomas Kinsella
sources.description: A modern translation of the Táin Bó Cúailnge, featuring Fergus's role.
sources.text: A modern translation of the Táin Bó Cúailnge, featuring Fergus's role.

sources.author: Jeffrey Gantz
sources.description: A collection of translated early Irish myths, including stories of Fergus mac Róich.
sources.text: A collection of translated early Irish myths, including stories of Fergus mac Róich.
